Ford GoBike System Data — Exploratory Analysis

Dataset: February 2019  |  Trips analyzed: 182,803  |  Source: Ford GoBike System Data

Introduction

This notebook explores individual rides made in the Ford GoBike bike-sharing system covering the greater San Francisco Bay area during February 2019. The dataset contains 16 features including trip duration, start/end times and locations, bike ID, user type, member birth year, gender, and equity program participation.

Questions to Explore:
  1. What is the distribution of trip durations?
  2. Who are the users (type, gender, age)?
  3. When do people ride (hour, day of week)?
  4. Does age predict trip duration?
  5. How do subscribers and customers differ in behavior?
  6. How do gender, user type, and time interact?

Preliminary Wrangling

Key steps: parsed datetime columns; created duration_min, age, hour_of_day, day_of_week; filtered ages outside 18–80; removed trips exceeding 180 minutes (extreme outliers, <0.5% of data).

FeatureTypeMissingNotes
duration_minfloat640Derived from duration_sec
user_typeobject0Subscriber / Customer
member_genderobject8,265Missing for unregistered customers
member_birth_yearfloat648,265Missing for unregistered customers
start_station_nameobject197Docked-elsewhere trips

Part 1: Univariate Exploration

Question: What are the individual distributions of key variables?

1.1 — Histogram: Trip Duration Distribution

Figure 1: Trip duration distribution — raw scale (left) and log scale (right). Mean and median annotated.

Observation: Trip duration is strongly right-skewed on the raw scale. The median (8.6 min) is well below the mean (12.1 min). On a log scale the distribution appears approximately normal, confirming log transformation is appropriate for downstream analysis.

1.2 — Bar Chart: Categorical Variable Distributions

Figure 2: Counts and percentages for User Type, Member Gender, and Bike Share for All program participation.

Observation: 89.1% of trips are by annual Subscribers — the system is primarily a commuter tool. Males account for 74.5% of trips with gender information. About 9.5% of trips are made under the Bike Share for All low-income equity program.

1.3 — Additional: Rides by Hour of Day

Figure 3: Trip starts by hour of day. Rush-hour peaks (8 AM and 5 PM) highlighted in red.

Observation: Two clear peaks at 8 AM (21,056 trips) and 5 PM (21,864 trips) confirm commuter-dominated usage. Activity is minimal between midnight and 5 AM.

Part 2: Bivariate Exploration

Question: How do pairs of variables relate? Does user type, gender, age, or day predict trip duration or timing?

2.1 — Scatter Plot: Age vs. Trip Duration

Figure 4: Rider age vs. log trip duration with linear regression line. Near-zero correlation confirmed.

Observation: Pearson r ≈ 0.007 — virtually no linear relationship between age and trip duration. The scatter shows a uniform cloud across all ages, suggesting trip purpose (commute vs. leisure) matters more than demographics.

2.2 — Box Plot: Duration by User Type and Gender

Figure 5: Box plots of trip duration by user type (left) and gender (right). Median values annotated.

Observation: Customers take significantly longer trips (median 13.2 min) than Subscribers (8.2 min) with higher variability. Gender differences are modest: female riders have slightly longer median durations (9.5 min) vs males (8.2 min).

2.3 — Bar Chart: Duration by Day of Week

Figure 6: Median trip duration by day of week. Weekend days highlighted in red.

Observation: Weekend trips are noticeably longer (~12–13 min) than weekday trips (~8–9 min). This confirms a leisure vs. commute behavioral split by day type.

2.4 — Heatmap: Trips by Hour and Day

Figure 7: Heatmap of trip counts by day of week and hour. Weekday rush hours clearly visible.

Observation: The heatmap reveals a bimodal commuter pattern Mon–Fri (dark red at 8 AM and 5 PM) versus a broad midday pattern on weekends. Tuesday and Wednesday appear to have the highest weekday ridership.

Part 3: Multivariate Exploration

Question: How do three or more variables interact? Is subscriber vs. customer behavior consistent across genders and times?

3.1 — Facet Plot: Duration by Gender and User Type

Figure 8: FacetGrid — trip duration distributions for each combination of gender and user type.

Observation: Subscriber distributions are consistently tight and short (~7–10 min) across all three genders. Customer distributions are broader and right-shifted regardless of gender. User type is the dominant factor; gender plays a secondary role.

3.2 — Facet Plot: Hourly Usage by User Type and Day Type

Figure 9: FacetGrid — average trips per hour by user type and weekday vs. weekend.

Observation: Subscribers show classic double-peaked commuter patterns on weekdays, shifting to single midday peaks on weekends. Customers show a consistent midday peak regardless of day type — confirming they are leisure/tourist users.

3.3 — Scatter with Multiple Encodings

Figure 10: Multi-encoding scatter — age vs. duration, colored by user type and shaped by gender.

Observation: Encoding both user type (color) and gender (shape) confirms that orange Customer points cluster in the upper half of the chart regardless of age or gender. User type is the strongest predictor of trip duration in this dataset.

Conclusions

  1. Trip duration is right-skewed (median 8.6 min); log transformation normalizes it well.
  2. The system is dominated by commuters: 89.1% of trips are by annual Subscribers with clear 8 AM / 5 PM peaks.
  3. User type is the strongest predictor of behavior: Subscribers take short efficient trips; Customers take longer leisure trips.
  4. Subscribers change behavior on weekends, shifting to a midday leisure pattern — but still shorter than Customer trips.
  5. Gender has modest effect: Females take slightly longer trips (~9.5 min) vs. males (~8.2 min).
  6. Age has negligible correlation with trip duration; user type matters far more.
